text:
OTOLARYNGOLOGY / HEAD AND NECK SURGERY
NEW PATIENT CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING: [Attending Otolaryngologist / Head and Neck Surgeon]
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: [Primary Care Physician]
REASON FOR REFERRAL: Persistent left throat discomfort, odynophagia, and new left level II cervical lymphadenopathy; CT neck concerning for oropharyngeal primary malignancy with ipsilateral nodal disease.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Left throat discomfort, left-sided otalgia, and firm left neck mass for approximately 4 months.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old East Asian woman, never-smoker, who presents for urgent evaluation at the request of her primary care physician after a CT scan of the neck demonstrated a suspicious 2.1 x 1.8 cm left level IIA cervical lymph node with loss of fatty hilum and internal heterogeneity, as well as asymmetric fullness at the left base of tongue. The radiologic impression was highly suspicious for primary oropharyngeal malignancy with ipsilateral nodal metastasis.

In terms of symptom chronology, she first developed a persistent, nagging left-sided throat discomfort and a sensation of something stuck in the back of her throat approximately 4 months ago. At that time, she was seen by her primary care physician and treated for presumed pharyngitis with a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate without improvement. Approximately 2 months thereafter -- now roughly 2 months ago -- she returned to her PCP reporting that the throat discomfort had persisted and had been joined by mild left-sided referred otalgia. At that visit, a firm, non-tender left level II lymph node approximately 2 cm in diameter was palpated, prompting urgent referral to our service.

She describes the throat discomfort as a dull pressure or fullness localized to the left side of the throat, worsened somewhat with swallowing, though she denies true dysphagia to solid foods at this point. She acknowledges mild odynophagia, mostly with drier textures and occasionally with liquids when she swallows vigorously. The left-sided otalgia is intermittent, rated 3/10, and she finds herself frequently pressing on the tragus as though to relieve it. She denies hoarseness, voice change, trismus, or change in her singing voice. She denies frank hemoptysis or blood-tinged sputum, though she reports that she occasionally notices a small streak of blood when she blows her nose, which she attributes to dry air. She denies fever, chills, night sweats, or significant unintentional weight loss (she estimates perhaps 1-2 pounds, which she attributes to reduced appetite secondary to the throat discomfort). She does not report any skin lesions, oral ulcerations, or other mucosal changes.

She denies any prior radiation to the head and neck. There is no prior history of oral cavity or pharyngeal lesions. She has never been evaluated by an otolaryngologist in the past. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections to her knowledge; she reports a limited number of lifetime sexual partners. She underwent HPV vaccination in her 30s as part of a healthcare initiative.

She is a high school music teacher and a trained vocalist; voice quality and swallowing function are of particular professional and personal importance to her.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Seasonal allergic rhinitis -- managed with loratadine as needed
2. Mild gastroesophageal reflux disease -- previously managed with dietary modifications; no current PPI use
3. Remote history of anxiety, now resolved without pharmacotherapy

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
1. Appendectomy (childhood, uncomplicated)
2. Wisdom tooth extraction (age ~20, uncomplicated)

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother: alive, hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Father: deceased, ischemic heart disease. No known family history of head and neck cancer, oral cavity cancer, or other squamous cell carcinomas. No known hereditary cancer syndromes in the family. One sibling: healthy.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Never-smoker (0 pack-year history). Minimal alcohol consumption, less than 1 drink per week, typically a glass of wine on occasion. No recreational drug use. Lives with her husband of 27 years and two adult children who are largely independent. Works full-time as a high school music teacher; finds her job deeply meaningful. Exercises regularly (walking, yoga). No occupational exposures to known carcinogens. Denies any asbestos or chemical exposure history. Born in Taiwan, has lived in the United States since early adulthood.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Loratadine 10 mg orally as needed for allergic rhinitis
2. No current prescription medications

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive for: persistent left throat discomfort/pressure (4 months), mild odynophagia, left-sided referred otalgia (intermittent), mild decreased appetite, intermittent epistaxis (minimal, right-sided, attributed to dry air).
Negative for: hoarseness, voice change, trismus, dysphagia to solids, significant weight loss (>5% body weight), fever, night sweats, frank hemoptysis, bleeding gums, oral ulcerations, skin lesions, nasal obstruction, nasal discharge, diplopia or visual changes, facial numbness or paresthesias, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, chest pain, or lymphadenopathy elsewhere.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished East Asian woman in no acute distress. Conversational, voice clear and melodic without hoarseness or muffling. Alert and oriented. ECOG performance status 1 (some mild fatigue).

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg; heart rate 76 bpm; respiratory rate 16; oxygen saturation 98% on room air; weight 62 kg; BMI approximately 22 kg/m2.

Head/Face: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial asymmetry. No parotid gland swelling or tenderness. Facial sensation intact to light touch V1-V3 bilaterally. No palpable pre- or post-auricular lymphadenopathy.

Ears: External auditory canals clear bilaterally; tympanic membranes intact with normal light reflex bilaterally. No middle ear effusion. No otorrhea. Cranial nerve VIII grossly intact.

Oral Cavity and Oropharynx (Indirect Mirror and Flexible Endoscopy, see below): Dentition fair; no caries or periodontal disease identified on gross inspection. Buccal mucosa, gingiva, hard palate, and anterior tongue without lesion. Floor of mouth unremarkable on visual inspection. Soft palate appears symmetric at rest and with phonation. Tonsillar fossae appear symmetric without mass or erythema.

Neck: Supple. RIGHT: No palpable lymphadenopathy. LEFT: A firm, non-tender, discrete lymph node is palpated in the left level IIA region, approximately 2 cm in greatest dimension. The node is mobile, does not appear matted, and does not appear fixed to underlying structures. No other cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No thyroid mass. No carotid bruit.

Nose: External nose unremarkable. Anterior rhinoscopy: mild inferior turbinate hypertrophy bilaterally; septum midline; no intranasal mass.

Nasopharynx and Larynx (Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy with Video Documentation):
-- Nasopharynx: Mucosa smooth, no mass, no asymmetry. Eustachian tube orifices patent bilaterally.
-- Oropharynx: Soft palate and uvula midline. Tonsillar fossae unremarkable bilaterally.
-- Base of Tongue / Hypopharynx: An EXOPHYTIC, FRIABLE, IRREGULAR LESION is identified arising from the LEFT BASE OF TONGUE. The lesion appears reddish-pink, somewhat erythematous, and irregular in surface contour. It measures approximately 2 cm in greatest dimension as assessed endoscopically. Contact bleeding was elicited with gentle scope passage. The right base of tongue is smooth and unremarkable. The valleculae appear clear. No extension to the epiglottis or pre-epiglottic space is appreciated endoscopically.
-- Supraglottis/Glottis: Epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds without mass or edema. Bilateral true vocal cords appear symmetric, white, and mobile with full abduction/adduction. No subglottic extension appreciated.
-- Hypopharynx: Piriform sinuses appear symmetric; no pooling of secretions; no mass.

Bimanual Palpation: Bimanual palpation of the left base of tongue was performed under topical anesthesia. A firm, indurated submucosal component is appreciated in the left tongue base extending toward the glossotonsillar sulcus. No crossing of midline palpated. The firmness of the left level IIA cervical lymph node is confirmed on bimanual exam.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No tongue deviation on protrusion. No shoulder weakness. Normal facial movement and sensation.

---

DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Imaging (reviewed): CT neck with contrast (performed at age 51 years, 11 months, reviewed in our department today):
- 2.1 x 1.8 cm left level IIA cervical lymph node with internal heterogeneity and loss of fatty hilum, highly suspicious for metastatic disease.
- Subtle asymmetric fullness of the left base of tongue.
- No contralateral lymphadenopathy identified.
- No distant disease assessed on this study.
Impression noted by radiology: highly suspicious for primary oropharyngeal malignancy with ipsilateral nodal metastasis.

Laboratory (from referring physician, available in chart):
- CBC, CMP reviewed -- no significant abnormalities noted. No leukocytosis. Creatinine and LFTs within normal limits.

---

ASSESSMENT
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking East Asian woman presenting with a 4-month history of left throat discomfort, referred left otalgia, and a firm left level IIA cervical lymph node. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy today reveals an exophytic, friable, contact-bleeding lesion arising from the left base of tongue, estimated at approximately 2 cm, with an associated 2 cm firm left level IIA node confirmed on both imaging and bimanual palpation.

The clinical and radiologic picture is highly suspicious for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, primary base of tongue. Given the patient's demographics -- never-smoker, East Asian woman in her early 50s -- and the endoscopic appearance of this lesion, HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC must be strongly considered as the leading diagnosis. The differential diagnosis at this point remains narrow; lymphoma is on the differential given the prominent nodal involvement, but the endoscopic appearance of the primary lesion is far more consistent with squamous cell carcinoma. Salivary gland malignancy (e.g., mucoepidermoid carcinoma or adenoid cystic carcinoma of the base of tongue) is possible but less likely given the gross morphology.

Histologic confirmation is required before any treatment planning proceeds.

---

PLAN

1. BIOPSY: The patient has been counseled regarding the suspicious nature of the left base of tongue lesion and the need for tissue diagnosis. She will be scheduled for direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia. At the time of surgery, systematic examination of the upper aerodigestive tract will be performed to assess extent of disease and rule out synchronous primaries. Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of the left level IIA cervical lymph node will be performed concurrently (or intraoperatively) for cytologic evaluation.

2. ADDITIONAL STAGING IMAGING: Cross-sectional imaging of the chest (CT chest with contrast, or PET-CT) will be obtained to evaluate for distant disease, pending and likely contingent on pathologic confirmation. We anticipate requesting PET-CT for comprehensive staging given the clinical scenario.

3. MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE: If malignancy is confirmed on biopsy, the case will be presented at our multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board. Medical oncology, radiation oncology, speech-language pathology, and dietetics consultations will be arranged as indicated.

4. PATIENT COUNSELING: Ms. Chen was informed of today's examination findings, including the discovery of an abnormal lesion at the left base of tongue. The findings were explained in detail using plain language; she was accompanied by her husband. The possibility of malignancy was discussed honestly and with compassion. She was informed that tissue biopsy is the necessary next step before any definitive statements about diagnosis or treatment can be made. She was understandably distressed but composed, asking thoughtful questions about timeline, prognosis, and the implications for her voice and swallowing function. She was reassured that these concerns will be central to our treatment planning process. She verbalized understanding and agreement with the proposed plan.

5. RETURN: Patient to be scheduled for direct laryngoscopy and biopsy within 1 week. She will be contacted with a confirmed surgical date by the end of the business day.

---

Time spent in direct patient contact and counseling: approximately 60 minutes.

[Electronically signed by Attending Otolaryngologist / Head and Neck Surgeon]
[Dictated and reviewed]
